metadata.title: Claude, On the Job.
metadata.color_semantics: claude fidelity: INK #3D3929; SPARK #D97757 the one accent. Do not retint.
metadata.greeting_note: HAI persona, shortest-form rule. 'Ciao' = Italian warmth, one word.
metadata.thesis: You're not behind the machine. You're what the machine is missing.
metadata.note: ILLUSTRATE LAW reel: UI at B00 (cold open with RESULT lines — the tier answer), B06 (verdict artifact), B07 (handoff), B08 (outro). Inner beats B01–B05 each use a distinct custom Remotion scene on cream stage. PREDICT at B05. COLD OPEN, SPARK-LINE, HANDOFF, OUTRO laws in force. Tier diagram (B01) is the visual anchor of the episode. House red line: never teaches evading a ban or integrity policy — teaches conducting AI, not competing with it.

beats.narration_text: … this is Liam, in for Bear. Are you too late to learn AI? No. Here's the picture that makes the answer clear: a four-tier model of what AI does, what it's replacing, and what stays permanently human. Tier 1 is the machine's territory now.

props.command: am I too late to learn AI?
output: no — the durable skills are yours
Tier 1 is the machine's: recall, syntax, retrieval
Tier 4 is yours: judgment, audit, distrust-calibration

beats.narration_text: The tier picture: four levels. Tier 1 is recall, syntax, retrieval — looking things up, writing boilerplate, translating between formats. That tier belongs to the machine now. Tier 2 is analysis: finding patterns, comparing options, surfacing implications. AI is very good at it but often needs your framing to do it well. Tier 3 is judgment — the consequential call. Tier 4 is relationship. AI does not hold those.

What's actually scarce right now is the person who can work at Tier 3 and 4 while leveraging Tier 1 and 2 through AI. Employers aren't paying extra for recall or boilerplate. They're paying for the person who takes AI's analysis and makes the consequential call — and who can maintain the relationship before and after.

props.heading: What's actually scarce right now is the person who can work at Tier 3 and 4 whil
props.body: Employers aren't paying extra for recall or boilerplate. They're paying for the person who takes AI's analysis and makes the consequential call — and who can maintain the relationship before and after.

beats.narration_text: The daily practice: one real work task per day run through Claude. Not a demo, not an experiment — an actual deliverable you were going to produce anyway. Over a week you develop calibration: what Claude does well in your field, where it needs your framing, where you still verify everything.

props.heading: The daily practice: one real work task per day run through Claude.
props.body: Not a demo, not an experiment — an actual deliverable you were going to produce anyway. Over a week you develop calibration: what Claude does well in your field, where it needs your framing, where you still verify everything.

beats.narration_text: What not to delegate: facts you sign your name to — you verify before the signature. Consequential judgment calls where you bear the cost of being wrong. Relationships where the other person needs to know they're talking to you. These aren't AI-assisted tasks. They're yours.

props.heading: What not to delegate: facts you sign your name to — you verify before the signat
props.body: Consequential judgment calls where you bear the cost of being wrong. Relationships where the other person needs to know they're talking to you. These aren't AI-assisted tasks. They're yours.
props.sparkLine: Your name on it = your judgment.

beats.narration_text: Commit: which of these four tasks is yours alone — meaning Claude should not produce the output? A: summarize this meeting's key decisions. B: draft the follow-up email. C: decide whether to fire the vendor. D: generate five options for the project timeline.

props.heading: Commit: which of these four tasks is yours alone — meaning Claude should not pro
props.body: B: draft the follow-up email. C: decide whether to fire the vendor. D: generate five options for the project timeline.

beats.narration_text: C. Firing a vendor is a consequential judgment call — you bear the cost, the relationship consequence, and the downstream effects. The other three are Tier 1 and 2 assist tasks. The line isn't whether Claude can produce something useful; it's whether you're the person accountable for the decision.

props.artifactTitle: Claude, On the Job — one card
artifactLines: The tier picture: Tier 1 is the machine's (recall, syntax). Tier 4 is yours (problem formulation, audit, distrust-calibration). Conduct, don't compete.
What's scarce: knowing what to ask, reading output critically, catching confabulations, deciding what reaches the human.
The daily loop: one real task, every output audited, one weak point named. That's judgment-building.
Never delegate: facts you sign your name to, judgment calls where you bear the consequence, your professional reputation.

beats.narration_text: Your move. The handoff prompt takes one real task from your day, runs it through Claude, and then asks you to evaluate what Claude got right, what it missed, and what you had to add. Do that for a week and you'll know your own calibration.

props.command: Help me with [one real task from today's work]. After you give me the output, I'm going to tell you what I think you got wrong or weak — and you tell me if I'm right.

beats.narration_text: Claude, On the Job. If the tier picture helps, subscribe. HAI. Liam, in for Bear.
